original_abstract: Research into the circular economy (CE) is vital for accelerating global sustainability transitions and enhancing climate change resilience. To date, however, scholarly discourse has been heavily skewed toward the Global North, leaving the unique challenges and opportunities of African contexts largely overlooked. This study addresses this imbalance by establishing a preliminary research agenda for CE development across the continent, identifying existing knowledge clusters, critical gaps, and the practical barriers to local research. Adopting a mixed-methods approach, we conducted a scoping review of 275 English and French articles from Google Scholar, complemented by quantitative and qualitative surveys with 38 industry experts actively managing CE projects in Africa. The results reveal that South Africa currently leads the continent’s CE research landscape, hosting the majority of publications and ongoing initiatives. This geographic concentration underscores an urgent need for country-specific studies elsewhere, particularly as much of the current literature treats Africa as a monolith rather than a diverse collection of distinct economies. Furthermore, our analysis indicates that existing research focuses disproportionately on material circulation and product longevity, while neglecting the critical CE principles of designing out waste and regenerating natural systems. By synthesizing these findings, this paper offers a targeted list of priority research topics. As the first attempt to systematically map a CE research agenda tailored to African contexts, this study provides a foundational roadmap for future scholarly and policy inquiry.
humanized_abstract: Research on the circular economy (CE) is essential to support and accelerate the transition to a more sustainable future and achieve climate change resilience. Current scholarly research focuses heavily on the Global North and often disregards the specific needs, barriers and opportunities of developing countries including Africa. This paper therefore initiates a continental CE research agenda, lists existing knowledge clusters and research gaps and specifies barriers to CE-related research in African countries. The methodology applies a scoping review on a total of 275 articles in English and French sourced from Google Scholar and qualitative and quantitative surveys among 38 companies in Africa managing their own CE-related projects. This research identifies South Africa as being at the forefront of CE-related research and publications on the continent. Due to the large number of publications in the country as well as on the continent related to CE in general and material loops and product reuse in particular, our study recommends that CE-related research must be carried out for individual countries on the continent to help address gaps in knowledge gaps, and barriers for each country specifically, because the majority of literature reviews concerning CE developments in Africa treat Africa as a single economy. While research has started exploring the application of business models addressing material loops and the reuse of products, the three cornerstone principles of the CE have not yet been comprehensively addressed in research related to African countries. In conclusion, this study compiles a specific list of research priorities to initiate further scholarly research and policy activities related to the development of the CE on the continent. This paper therefore represents the first attempt to conduct a review of a CE-related research agenda for countries in Africa.
